text: Although highly efficient adsorptive separation of gas mixtures of increasing complexity has become a focal point in chemical engineering, achieving, via molecular sieving-type adsorbents, high selectivity and high capacity remains a persistent challenge. We present oxyfluoride metal-organic framework UTSA-400, [Ni(WO₂F₄)(pyz)₂] (pyz = pyrazine), composed of a stable 3D structure containing open 1D micropores that can selectively bind propylene molecules at the optimal strength while rejecting propane. The oxide/fluoride pairs on the surface of the 3D structure significantly increase the host-guest interactions that enhance the propylene uptake. The adsorbed propane is rejected because the host framework adjusts its geometry so that propane cannot be accommodated inside the pores. In the pores of UTSA-400, propylene is adsorbed at nearly saturated levels under ambient conditions while reaching high packing density. Detailed insights into the nature of the strong binding of propylene in the 1D micropores were gained by combining in situ IR spectroscopy measurements and dispersion corrected density functional theory (DFT) studies. Breakthrough experiments demonstrate that polymer grade (>99.5%) propylene can be produced directly by UTSA-400 while maintaining high dynamic productivity.
